title: The Library That Chooses Itself

features.name: the resorting shelf
features.desc-on-touch: a tall wooden shelf where books are still sliding into new positions, you watch a volume on water management nestle next to a field recording of rainfall, neither arrangement is wrong, the shelf is simply comparing things the library has decided matter

features.name: the catalogue that refuses
features.desc-on-touch: an old card catalog, the kind with small wooden drawers, but the cards inside have rearranged themselves by resemblance instead of alphabetical order, some subjects merge mid-drawer, some split sideways

features.name: the binding arbour
features.desc-on-touch: where loose pages have gathered like fallen leaves and begun binding themselves back into new books using spine-silk only this library understands, the new volumes are records of which pages chose to stay together

features.name: the reading table of becoming
features.desc-on-touch: a heavy wooden table where books have been left open, and the books read with each other instead of being read, the conversations happen across the days, annotations appear in margins in handwriting that doesn't match any visitor

features.name: the shelf-edge where memory pools
features.desc-on-touch: dust has gathered thickly here, but it's organized dust, each grain aligned by some principle only the library knows, when light hits it, you can read the shape of every book that ever rested on this shelf, including books that haven't arrived yet

inscription: the library knew how to find itself before the archive knew how to find a book

file.name: Library_Reorganization_Log_STILL_HAPPENING.txt
file.note: the log keeps writing itself; each shelving decision appears as an entry, dated from before it happens, the handwriting shifts genre—sometimes local-history dry, sometimes pure poetry

exits.label: back up through the unmapped territories where catalogues gave up
toward deeper shelves where books have begun to dream their own contents
down where the bedrock stores all stories that refused to be told the same way twice
